content.text: You need to understand and learn about pi mono thinking message styling.

content.thinking: The user wants to understand how pi handles "mono thinking" message styling. Let me start by reading the relevant documentation to understand pi's architecture, themes, and TUI components.

content.text: I'll start by reading the pi documentation to understand how message styling, themes, and TUI components work.

> pi can create themes. Ask it to build one for your setup.

# Themes

Themes are JSON files that define colors for the TUI.

## Table of Contents

- [Locations](#locations)
- [Selecting a Theme](#selecting-a-theme)
- [Creating a Custom Theme](#creating-a-custom-theme)
- [Theme Format](#theme-format)
- [Color Tokens](#color-tokens)
- [Color Values](#color-values)
- [Tips](#tips)

## Locations

Pi loads themes from:

- Built-in: `dark`, `light`
- Global: `~/.pi/agent/themes/*.json`
- Project: `.pi/themes/*.json`
- Packages: `themes/` directories or `pi.themes` entries in `package.json`
- Settings: `themes` array with files or directories
- CLI: `--theme <path>` (repeatable)

Disable discovery with `--no-themes`.

## Selecting a Theme

Select a theme via `/settings` or in `settings.json`:

```json
{
  "theme": "my-theme"
}
```

On first run, pi detects your terminal background and defaults to `dark` or `light`.

## Creating a Custom Theme

1. Create a theme file:

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.pi/agent/themes
vim ~/.pi/agent/themes/my-theme.json
```

2. Define the theme with all required colors (see [Color Tokens](#color-tokens)):

```json
{
  "$schema": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/badlogic/pi-mono/main/packages/coding-agent/src/modes/interactive/theme/theme-schema.json",
  "name": "my-theme",
  "vars": {
    "primary": "#00aaff",
    "secondary": 242
  },
  "colors": {
    "accent": "primary",
    "border": "primary",
    "borderAccent": "#00ffff",
    "borderMuted": "secondary",
    "success": "#00ff00",
    "error": "#ff0000",
    "warning": "#ffff00",
    "muted": "secondary",
    "dim": 240,
    "text": "",
    "thinkingText": "secondary",
    "selectedBg": "#2d2d30",
    "userMessageBg": "#2d2d30",
    "userMessageText": "",
    "customMessageBg": "#2d2d30",
    "customMessageText": "",
    "customMessageLabel": "primary",
    "toolPendingBg": "#1e1e2e",
    "toolSuccessBg": "#1e2e1e",
    "toolErrorBg": "#2e1e1e",
    "toolTitle": "primary",
    "toolOutput": "",
    "mdHeading": "#ffaa00",
    "mdLink": "primary",
    "mdLinkUrl": "secondary",
    "mdCode": "#00ffff",
    "mdCodeBlock": "",
    "mdCodeBlockBorder": "secondary",
    "mdQuote": "secondary",
    "mdQuoteBorder": "secondary",
    "mdHr": "secondary",
    "mdListBullet": "#00ffff",
    "toolDiffAdded": "#00ff00",
    "toolDiffRemoved": "#ff0000",
    "toolDiffContext": "secondary",
    "syntaxComment": "secondary",
    "syntaxKeyword": "primary",
    "syntaxFunction": "#00aaff",
    "syntaxVariable": "#ffaa00",
    "syntaxString": "#00ff00",
    "syntaxNumber": "#ff00ff",
    "syntaxType": "#00aaff",
    "syntaxOperator": "primary",
    "syntaxPunctuation": "secondary",
    "thinkingOff": "secondary",
    "thinkingMinimal": "primary",
    "thinkingLow": "#00aaff",
    "thinkingMedium": "#00ffff",
    "thinkingHigh": "#ff00ff",
    "thinkingXhigh": "#ff0000",
    "bashMode": "#ffaa00"
  }
}
```

3. Select the theme via `/settings`.

**Hot reload:** When you edit the currently active custom theme file, pi reloads it automatically for immediate visual feedback.

## Theme Format

```json
{
  "$schema": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/badlogic/pi-mono/main/packages/coding-agent/src/modes/interactive/theme/theme-schema.json",
  "name": "my-theme",
  "vars": {
    "blue": "#0066cc",
    "gray": 242
  },
  "colors": {
    "accent": "blue",
    "muted": "gray",
    "text": "",
    ...
  }
}
```

- `name` is required and must be unique.
- `vars` is optional. Define reusable colors here, then reference them in `colors`.
- `colors` must define all 51 required tokens.

The `$schema` field enables editor auto-completion and validation.

## Color Tokens

Every theme must define all 51 color tokens. There are no optional colors.

### Core UI (11 colors)

| Token | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `accent` | Primary accent (logo, selected items, cursor) |
| `border` | Normal borders |
| `borderAccent` | Highlighted borders |
| `borderMuted` | Subtle borders (editor) |
| `success` | Success states |
| `error` | Error states |
| `warning` | Warning states |
| `muted` | Secondary text |
| `dim` | Tertiary text |
| `text` | Default text (usually `""`) |
| `thinkingText` | Thinking block text |

### Backgrounds & Content (11 colors)

| Token | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `selectedBg` | Selected line background |
| `userMessageBg` | User message background |
| `userMessageText` | User message text |
| `customMessageBg` | Extension message background |
| `customMessageText` | Extension message text |
| `customMessageLabel` | Extension message label |
| `toolPendingBg` | Tool box (pending) |
| `toolSuccessBg` | Tool box (success) |
| `toolErrorBg` | Tool box (error) |
| `toolTitle` | Tool title |
| `toolOutput` | Tool output text |

### Markdown (10 colors)

| Token | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `mdHeading` | Headings |
| `mdLink` | Link text |
| `mdLinkUrl` | Link URL |
| `mdCode` | Inline code |
| `mdCodeBlock` | Code block content |
| `mdCodeBlockBorder` | Code block fences |
| `mdQuote` | Blockquote text |
| `mdQuoteBorder` | Blockquote border |
| `mdHr` | Horizontal rule |
| `mdListBullet` | List bullets |

### Tool Diffs (3 colors)

| Token | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `toolDiffAdded` | Added lines |
| `toolDiffRemoved` | Removed lines |
| `toolDiffContext` | Context lines |

### Syntax Highlighting (9 colors)

| Token | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `syntaxComment` | Comments |
| `syntaxKeyword` | Keywords |
| `syntaxFunction` | Function names |
| `syntaxVariable` | Variables |
| `syntaxString` | Strings |
| `syntaxNumber` | Numbers |
| `syntaxType` | Types |
| `syntaxOperator` | Operators |
| `syntaxPunctuation` | Punctuation |

### Thinking Level Borders (6 colors)

Editor border colors indicating thinking level (visual hierarchy from subtle to prominent):

| Token | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `thinkingOff` | Thinking off |
| `thinkingMinimal` | Minimal thinking |
| `thinkingLow` | Low thinking |
| `thinkingMedium` | Medium thinking |
| `thinkingHigh` | High thinking |
| `thinkingXhigh` | Extra high thinking |

### Bash Mode (1 color)

| Token | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `bashMode` | Editor border in bash mode (`!` prefix) |

### HTML Export (optional)

The `export` section controls colors for `/export` HTML output. If omitted, colors are derived from `userMessageBg`.

```json
{
  "export": {
    "pageBg": "#18181e",
    "cardBg": "#1e1e24",
    "infoBg": "#3c3728"
  }
}
```

## Color Values

Four formats are supported:

| Format | Example | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| Hex | `"#ff0000"` | 6-digit hex RGB |
| 256-color | `39` | xterm 256-color palette index (0-255) |
| Variable | `"primary"` | Reference to a `vars` entry |
| Default | `""` | Terminal's default color |

### 256-Color Palette

- `0-15`: Basic ANSI colors (terminal-dependent)
- `16-231`: 6×6×6 RGB cube (`16 + 36×R + 6×G + B` where R,G,B are 0-5)
- `232-255`: Grayscale ramp

### Terminal Compatibility

Pi uses 24-bit RGB colors. Most modern terminals support this (iTerm2, Kitty, WezTerm, Windows Terminal, VS Code). For older terminals with only 256-color support, pi falls back to the nearest approximation.

Check truecolor support:

```bash
echo $COLORTERM  # Should output "truecolor" or "24bit"
```

## Tips

**Dark terminals:** Use bright, saturated colors with higher contrast.

**Light terminals:** Use darker, muted colors with lower contrast.

**Color harmony:** Start with a base palette (Nord, Gruvbox, Tokyo Night), define it in `vars`, and reference consistently.

**Testing:** Check your theme with different message types, tool states, markdown content, and long wrapped text.

**VS Code:** Set `terminal.integrated.minimumContrastRatio` to `1` for accurate colors.

## Examples

See the built-in themes:
- [dark.json](../src/modes/interactive/theme/dark.json)
- [light.json](../src/modes/interactive/theme/light.json)
Now let me look at the source code related to thinking message rendering:

Now I have a thorough understanding. Let me summarize everything about pi mono thinking message styling.

---

Here's a comprehensive breakdown of **pi mono thinking message styling**:

## How Thinking Messages Work in Pi

There are **two distinct visual channels** for thinking in pi:

### 1. Thinking Content in Assistant Messages (Text Rendering)

When a model returns a `thinking` content block, it's rendered by `AssistantMessageComponent`:

**File:** `dist/modes/interactive/components/assistant-message.js`

Two modes:
- **Hidden mode** (`hideThinkingBlock = true`): Shows a static italic label like *"Thinking..."* using:
  ```ts
  theme.italic(theme.fg("thinkingText", hiddenThinkingLabel))
  ```
- **Visible mode** (default): Renders the thinking text as **Markdown** with a default text style override:
  ```ts
  new Markdown(content.thinking.trim(), 1, 0, markdownTheme, {
      color: (text) => theme.fg("thinkingText", text),
      italic: true,
  })
  ```

The `Markdown` component's 5th argument is a `defaultTextStyle` object that can override:
- `color` — a function wrapping text in a foreground color
- `italic` — applies italic styling to all thinking text
- `bold` / `strikethrough` / `bgColor` — also available but not used for thinking

**Theme color token:** `thinkingText` — controls the text color of thinking blocks. In the dark theme it's `gray` (#808080), in light it's `mediumGray` (#6c6c6c).

### 2. Editor Border Color (Visual Thinking Level Indicator)

The input editor's border color changes based on the current thinking level, providing an always-visible indicator:

**File:** `dist/modes/interactive/interactive-mode.js` — `updateEditorBorderColor()`
```ts
updateEditorBorderColor() {
    if (this.isBashMode) {
        this.editor.borderColor = theme.getBashModeBorderColor();
    } else {
        const level = this.session.thinkingLevel || "off";
        this.editor.borderColor = theme.getThinkingBorderColor(level);
    }
}
```

**`Theme.getThinkingBorderColor(level)`** maps levels to theme color tokens:

| Level | Theme Token | Dark Theme Value | Light Theme Value | Meaning |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|-------------------|---------|
| `off` | `thinkingOff` | `#505050` (dark gray) | `#b0b0b0` (light gray) | Thinking disabled |
| `minimal` | `thinkingMinimal` | `#6e6e6e` | `#767676` | ~1k tokens |
| `low` | `thinkingLow` | `#5f87af` (steel blue) | `#547da7` (blue) | ~2k tokens |
| `medium` | `thinkingMedium` | `#81a2be` (soft blue) | `#5a8080` (teal) | ~8k tokens |
| `high` | `thinkingHigh` | `#b294bb` (muted purple) | `#875f87` (plum) | ~16k tokens |
| `xhigh` | `thinkingXhigh` | `#d183e8` (bright purple) | `#8b008b` (dark magenta) | ~32k tokens |

The colors progress from **subtle/muted** → **prominent/vivid** as thinking level increases, creating a visual hierarchy.

### 3. Footer Thinking Level Display

The footer shows the current thinking level alongside the model name:
```ts
const thinkingLevel = state.thinkingLevel || "off";
rightSide = thinkingLevel === "off" 
    ? `${modelName} • thinking off` 
    : `${modelName} • ${thinkingLevel}`;
```

### Theme Customization

All thinking styling can be customized in a theme JSON file (`~/.pi/agent/themes/*.json`):

```json
{
  "colors": {
    "thinkingText": "#808080",       // Thinking block text color
    "thinkingOff": "#505050",        // Editor border when thinking off
    "thinkingMinimal": "#6e6e6e",    // Editor border at minimal
    "thinkingLow": "#5f87af",        // Editor border at low
    "thinkingMedium": "#81a2be",     // Editor border at medium
    "thinkingHigh": "#b294bb",       // Editor border at high
    "thinkingXhigh": "#d183e8"       // Editor border at xhigh
  }
}
```

### Summary Architecture

```
Thinking Level Setting
    │
    ├──► Editor Border Color (getThinkingBorderColor)
    │    └── DynamicBorder ──────────- renders ──► colored "───" border
    │
    ├──► Footer Status Line
    │    └── "model-name • thinking: medium"
    │
    └──► LLM Request (budget_tokens)
         └── Response thinking block
              └── AssistantMessageComponent
                   ├── Hidden: Text("Thinking...", italic, thinkingText color)
                   └── Visible: Markdown(text, { color: thinkingText, italic: true })
```

The key insight is that **thinking has two visual dimensions**: (1) the **editor border** color communicates the *current level setting* at all times, and (2) the **thinking block text** in assistant messages shows the *actual thinking output* in a muted, italic style that visually distinguishes it from regular assistant text.

Let me re-examine the code. When thinking is visible, it's rendered as:

```ts
new Markdown(content.thinking.trim(), 1, 0, this.markdownTheme, {
    color: (text) => theme.fg("thinkingText", text),
    italic: true,
})
```

The Markdown component's 5th parameter is `defaultTextStyle` which can have `color`, `italic`, `bold`, `strikethrough`, `underline`, and... `bgColor`.

But the thinking content doesn't set `bgColor`. So where's the background coming from?

Wait - let me re-read the Markdown render method more carefully. The padding and background logic:

1. Content lines are padded to full width with spaces
2. If `bgFn` is set, it applies background; otherwise just pads with spaces

So thinking text should NOT have a background. But the user sees one.

Actually, wait. The `defaultTextStyle` has `italic: true`. The `theme.italic()` uses chalk's italic. In some terminals, italic text might render differently. But that shouldn't give a background.

Let me reconsider. The thinking text is rendered through the Markdown parser. If the thinking text contains things like code blocks (with ```), the markdown theme's `codeBlock` and `code` functions could apply styling. But looking at `getMarkdownTheme()`, none of them apply background colors - they all use `theme.fg()`.

Actually, I wonder if the user is seeing the **regular assistant text** (not thinking) with what appears to be a background. Or perhaps the thinking content renders differently because of how the Markdown component handles `italic: true` combined with `color`.

Wait, actually I should look at this from a completely different angle. The user says:

1. They see a message with text like "The user is saying that the thinking text has a background color..."
2. This message has a background color behind it
3. After this message is done, subsequent messages don't have the background
4. They have "hide thinking" disabled

Actually, let me re-read the user's description more carefully. They say "this kind" of message - the message content looks like internal reasoning ("Let me look more carefully...", "Let me re-read..."). These are indeed thinking blocks.

The key question is: what's rendering them with a background?

Let me look at whether the `Markdown` component with `defaultTextStyle.italic: true` and `defaultTextStyle.color` somehow creates a visual background effect in their terminal. 

Actually, I think I need to look at whether the `defaultTextStyle` being passed includes something I'm missing. Let me re-read the assistant-message.js code:

```js

    color: (text) => theme.fg("thinkingText", text),
    italic: true,

```

The 5th arg is `{ color: fn, italic: true }`. No `bgColor`. No `bold`. No `underline`. No `strikethrough`.

But wait - the Markdown component's `render()` method pads lines to full width. And the TUI appends `\x1b[0m` at the end of each line. The thinking text color is `\x1b[38;2;128;128;128m` (gray). When this gets applied to the text, and then the line is padded with spaces (which would be in default color), and then the TUI appends `\x1b[0m`...

Hmm, but the `defaultTextStyle.color` wraps ALL text in the markdown, including the padding spaces. Wait no - looking at the code again:

```js
// In render():
const lineWithMargins = leftMargin + line + rightMargin;
if (bgFn) {
    contentLines.push(applyBackgroundToLine(lineWithMargins, width, bgFn));
} else {
    const visibleLen = visibleWidth(lineWithMargins);
    const paddingNeeded = Math.max(0, width - visibleLen);
    contentLines.push(lineWithMargins + " ".repeat(paddingNeeded));
}
```

The `line` already has the color applied via `applyDefaultStyle()` and individual token styling. The margins and padding spaces are NOT colored. So the thinking text would have gray foreground, but the padding spaces would be default color. This should look normal.

Hmm, unless... the `defaultStylePrefix` mechanism is applying the thinking color to content that follows, like code blocks or other styled elements, and that creates a visual difference.

Actually, I think I should just focus on what the user wants: they want thinking text to be plain gray text without any visual effects. The current rendering uses `italic: true` which the user might be interpreting as a visual effect/background (italic rendering in some terminals can look like slanted text with a background).

But the user explicitly said "it's not italic but it is having like background color behind the text."

1. The Markdown component renders thinking text with `paddingX=1` and pads to full width
2. The thinking text is gray (#808080) on a dark terminal background
3. The regular assistant text is default color (typically white/light)

Wait, actually let me reconsider. The user said "it's having like background color behind the text." In many terminal emulators, when text has a foreground color set, the spaces between/around characters might visually appear to have a background because of anti-aliasing or sub-pixel rendering. But that's unlikely to be noticeable.

Another possibility: the thinking text is wrapped in Markdown, and if the thinking text contains something like `>` (blockquotes) or `` ` `` (code), those get special styling from the markdown theme that might include a visual distinction.

OR - and this is a key insight - the thinking text often has indented/structured content that the Markdown parser interprets as code blocks. A code block in a terminal might visually appear to have a "background" because of the different foreground color creating a visual band across the full width.

```bash

```
